EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty winds occurred across much of the state of Utah at the tail end of the month. A few isolated severe gusts were observed on June 28th, with stronger winds on June 29th. In addition, a Russian olive tree was knocked over at the north side of the Salt Lake City International airport, and fell onto a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures along with occasional heavy rain led to continued flooding across the state of Utah. Major rivers that experienced flooding included the Weber River, the Sevier River, and the South Fork of the Ogden River. Note that this episode began in May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork of the Ogden River remained above flood stage for much of the first half of June, peaking at 1318 cfs on June 8th, before finally dropping below flood stage for the rest of the season in mid-June. Most of the damage was already done in earlier parts of the season, but this flooding continued to impact summer homes and campgrounds along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures along with occasional heavy rain led to continued flooding across the state of Utah. Major rivers that experienced flooding included the Weber River, the Sevier River, and the South Fork of the Ogden River. Note that this episode began in May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sevier River flooded in Sanpete County, with many roads damaged in the Gunnison and Centerfield area. Water was a foot deep along parts of the Clarion Road, damaging both the road and a nearby hunting club. There was extensive flooding along the Gunnison Farmers Freeway, with some road and bridge damage in that area. Damage was also reported along River Bridge Road in the Fayette area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures along with occasional heavy rain led to continued flooding across the state of Utah. Major rivers that experienced flooding included the Weber River, the Sevier River, and the South Fork of the Ogden River. Note that this episode began in May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weber River continued to flood through early June, and became significantly worse when a levee near Plain City broke on June 9th. These flood waters impacted barns and houses along the river, though the houses were spared from interior damage. Many fields and yards across the area were inundated by significant amounts of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed across northern Utah on May 13th, with the greatest impact being that these storms helped to fuel river flooding that had already begun due to snowmelt. However, one of these storms also produced a severe wind gust along Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Interstate 80 sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a maximum wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed across northern Utah on May 22nd, some of which produced strong wind gusts and penny-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny-sized hail was reported in Lehi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt and heavy rain caused flooding on the Blacksmith Fork River. This minor flooding caused several road closures around the towns of Logan and Millville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blacksmith Fork River reached flood stage on May 8th, with a peak flow of 978 cfs, above the flood flow of 934 cfs. Several roads around Logan were flooded and closed, including areas near 600 West and Park Avenue, and areas near 1700 South and US Routes 89 and 91. In addition, areas around the town of Millville received minor flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along the Alvey Wash and Harris Wash, washing out Hole-in-the-Rock Road and Alvey Wash Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few rivers across Utah continued to flood into the month of July, before finally receding in mid-July. The major rivers that continued to flood included the Logan River and the Duchesne River. Note that this episode began in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spring Creek near the town of Providence, Utah continued to experienced flooding through the beginning of July. Another clogged culvert along the creek washed out a road in early July, but otherwise additional damage was minimal as waters began to recede and impacts were further mitigated. Note that this event began in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few rivers across Utah continued to flood into the month of July, before finally receding in mid-July. The major rivers that continued to flood included the Logan River and the Duchesne River. Note that this episode began in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued on the Upper Weber River in early July, with the Weber at Oakley gauge peaking at 3390 cfs on July 1st. Much of this flooding up to Rockport Reservoir was mitigated, but once the Rockport Reservoir was filled, it increased the challenge for people trying to manage the flood threat downstream. At the Camper World downstream in Coalville, many of the 158 RV pads in the park were covered with water, as water flowed from canals in adjoining fields and through broken areas of a berm around the park. The park was evacuated except for employees. Other areas downstream of Rockport Reservoir also experienced minor flooding. Note that this event began in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few rivers across Utah continued to flood into the month of July, before finally receding in mid-July. The major rivers that continued to flood included the Logan River and the Duchesne River. Note that this episode began in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sevier River once again experienced flooding in the month of July, but this time it was areas of Millard County that received the worst of the damage. The access road between Highways 50 and 257 was closed due to water running over the roadway. Water from Sevier River also flowed over portions of Highways 6 and 50, but did not result in any damage or closures. Flooding occurred at various points on the Sevier River channel from Leamington through Lynndyl, in the Delta and Deseret areas, and all the way to Sevier Lake. A few different county roads and bridges were closed or damaged, including the Leamington Bridge in Leamington and the access road to the Gunnison Massacre Historical Site west of Hinckley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 12 inches were measured above the 8000 foot level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and vigorous Pacific trough produced significant snowfall with windy conditions in the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 8 inches was measured above the 8000 foot level.  Winds gusted 25 to 45 mph and produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold and moist upper low tracked across northern Utah and produced significant snowfall in the northern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall accumulations of 4 to 9 inches were measured throughout the area.  Outdoor activities such as camping were disrupted by the snowfall during the Memorial Day period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a break from thunderstorms in early September, moisture returned to much of Utah for the 2nd week of the month. These storms produced flash flooding in southern Utah and an isolated severe wind gust in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Utah Department of Transportation reported a flash flood near Rockville, with water flowing across State Route 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist storm system entered northern Utah on November 12th, with the heaviest snow falling in the mountains of far northern Utah. Following the storm, a fatal avalanche occurred in Little Cottonwood Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tony Grove Lake SNOTEL received 2.10 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 21 inches of new snow for the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist storm system entered northern Utah on November 12th, with the heaviest snow falling in the mountains of far northern Utah. Following the storm, a fatal avalanche occurred in Little Cottonwood Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two expert skiers were traveling along the well-known terrain of Alta Ski Lifts and Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort on November 13th; however, the resorts were not open yet, and the snowpack was very unstable, with considerable to high avalanche danger. When the two skiers reached Gad Valley, one of them triggered an avalanche, which carried him approximately 800 feet through steep rocky terrain and over a small cliff. The victim, a 38-year-old male, was only partially buried, but had already passed away when emergency crews reached the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm system moved through Utah in the first week of November. This storm brought heavy snow to parts of northern Utah, as well as isolated gusty winds to parts of central and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With strong prefrontal winds on the morning of November 4th, the Cedar City ASOS recorded a maximum wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Utah on November 18th. Ahead of this front, strong southerly winds were observed across west central and southwest Utah. The baroclinic zone itself stalled in far northern Utah for several hours, allowing heavy snowfall to occur in the Cache Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US-6 @ Eureka sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 64 mph, as well as several other gusts greater than 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: UTAHS DIXIE AND ZION NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a precursor for what would become a very strong wind event across much of Utah on December 1st, northerly winds began to gust across parts of far western Utah on November 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both the Badger Spring RAWS and White Reef RAWS recorded strong northerly wind gusts beginning on November 30th, with a maximum wind gust on the 30th of 72 mph at the Badger Spring RAWS. Note that this event continued into December.

CZ_NAME: UTAHS DIXIE AND ZION NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty winds developed in Washington County in far southwest Utah. The strongest of these winds occurred in the narrow gaps across the county due to a tightening northerly pressure gradient.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White Reef RAWS recorded strong gap winds, with a maximum recorded wind gust of 63 mph, as well as gusts above 50 mph for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty winds developed in Washington County in far southwest Utah. The strongest of these winds occurred in the narrow gaps across the county due to a tightening northerly pressure gradient.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to gusty winds in the lower elevation gaps, strong winds also occurred in the higher elevations of southwest Utah. The White Reef RAWS measured the strongest winds across the area, with a maximum recorded wind gust of 63 mph.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a precursor for what would become a very strong wind event across much of Utah on December 1st, northerly winds began to gust across parts of far western Utah on November 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Granite sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet was the first sensor in the Great Salt Lake Desert to record strong wind gusts with this storm. A wind gust of 64 mph was recorded just before midnight. Note that this event continued into December.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Salt Lake Valley, followed by lake effect snow through the morning of November 5th. Snowfall accumulations across the area included 9 inches of new snow in Cottonwood Heights, 8 inches of snow in Upper Millcreek, and 7 inches in both Sandy and the Upper Avenues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved through the Intermountain West on November 25th. While the best moisture and instability remained north of Utah, strong westerly winds did surface across the far northern parts of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chepeta RAWS recorded a maximum wind gust of 83 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system moved into the Great Basin on April 7th, bringing strong southwesterly winds to southern and central Utah. As the storm moved through Utah, it produced heavy snowfall in both the mountains and valleys across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St. George Municipal Airport AWOS recorded more than an hour of sustained winds greater than 40 mph, with gusts generally in the 50 to 55 mph range. During this period of time, the airport often had visibilities of 0.25 miles or less due to significant amounts of blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The windy April across Utah continued on the 13th, when another system moved through the area. The strongest winds were prefrontal winds across the western half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CWOP sensor in Lehi recorded a maximum wind gust of 63 mph with the passage of the cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold front moved through northern Utah, initiating thunderstorms across the area, a couple of which produced severe wind gusts. In addition, lightning struck the National Weather Service Doppler radar on Promontory Point, causing damage and temporarily putting the radar out of service.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the KMTX WSR-88D, operated by the National Weather Service Salt Lake City Weather Forecast Office, and located at Promontory Point. The lightning strike immediately disabled the radar, and it was out of service for almost 24 hours. Some wiring and two circuit boards had to be replaced. In addition, 16 panels of the radome were damaged and had to be replaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front created several types of active weather across the Beehive State, including strong gusty winds in western Utah, heavy snow in northern Utah, and severe thunderstorms in both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts included 68 mph at Centerville, 63 mph in West Bountiful, 62 mph at Antelope Island, and 58 mph in South Ogden.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall generally ranged from 4 to 12 inches across the area.  Locally higher amounts included 18 inches at the Hickerson Park SNOTEL site.  Wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass moved into Utah for the beginning of February, and the strong pressure gradient associated with this arctic front created gusty canyon winds in both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty easterly canyon winds occurred across the northern Wasatch Front; two different sensors in Centerville recorded wind gusts of greater than 58 mph, with a maximum gust of 63 mph.

CZ_NAME: UTAHS DIXIE AND ZION NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass moved into Utah for the beginning of February, and the strong pressure gradient associated with this arctic front created gusty canyon winds in both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White Reef RAWS recorded strong northerly canyon winds for a few straight hours, with a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High releases were necessary for water control on Flaming Gorge Reservoir which created high flows downstream of the dam through early July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High releases were necessary to alleviate inflated inflow into Flaming Gorge Reservoir due to heavy runoff from melting snow that occurred from the record snow pack in the Central Rockies. The high releases created low land flooding below the reservoir, caused some bank erosion and impacted recreation along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last of the record snowpack across the central Rocky Mountains continued to melt into early July and caused extended high flows and river flooding along the Green River along the west edge of Grand County through the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areal flood warnings remained in effect for the first week of July along the Green River in Western Grand County, including the town of Green River. The flood stage at Green River was topped on July 2nd, where it remained for the next 5 days before slowly falling and eventually went below bank level by July 15th. Mitigation work by Grand County was needed through the first week of July to keep water off the city golf course and adjacent state park. Erosion of river banks continued during the high flows and flood waters remained over low lying ranch and farm lands near the river.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low dug south into southern Utah and Nevada on November 30th and December 1st. Combined with high pressure centered over Wyoming, a strong pressure gradient developed across much of Utah. This created strong northeasterly canyon and downslope winds across several parts of the state. These winds caused damage across the Wasatch Front, with most of the damage occurring along the northern Wasatch Front. In addition to the winds, heavy snow fell in the mountains of central and southern Utah as the low moved through the area. Note that this episode began in November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty winds continued across the Great Salt Lake Desert and Mountains through the morning of December 1st. Peak recorded wind gusts included 67 mph at Lower Cedar Mountain, 65 mph at the Utah Test and Training Range Thermal Treatment Unit sensor, 62 mph at Camel Back Mountain and Gunnison Island, and 58 mph at the Rosebud RAWS. Note that this event began in November.

Maximum recorded wind gusts included 69 mph at the University of Utah and 63 mph at the Mouth of Parley's. More than 50 large trees were uprooted, and other damage included branches knocked off trees, damage to business awnings, and damage to residential roofs. In addition, a middle school student in Salt Lake City was seriously injured when a large tree branch fell on him as he was walking to school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong post-frontal downslope winds were observed in Castle Country, primarily impacting the town of Ferron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong downslope winds damaged a manufactured home in Ferron, blowing off the roof of the trailer. In addition, winds were strong many miles to the south of Ferron, with a maximum wind gust of 72 mph recorded at the Fremont Junction sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough produced significant snowfall in the mountains of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 4 to 9 inches above the 8000 foot level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The windy April across Utah continued on the 13th, when another system moved through the area. The strongest winds were prefrontal winds across the western half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fish Springs sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a maximum wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of warmer temperatures and heavy rainfall caused flooding across many creeks and rivers across northern Utah. A few of the most impacted waterways included the Blacksmith Fork River, the Weber River, and Chalk Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Weber River all the way to Plain City. The Weber River at Plain City gauge peaked at 4761 cfs, above its flood stage of 4713 cfs. While flooding threatened some homes in Riverdale, most of the damage was restricted to buildings  in agricultural areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front initiated widespread thunderstorms across northern Utah, with many of them producing severe wind gusts and causing property damage. In addition, some strong synoptic-scale winds were observed behind the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sites near Wendover recorded strong wind gusts with this thunderstorm, with peak wind gusts of 71 mph at the Wendover Point of Entry, 65 mph at the I-80 at Milepost 1 sensor, and 59 mph at Wendover Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front initiated widespread thunderstorms across northern Utah, with many of them producing severe wind gusts and causing property damage. In addition, some strong synoptic-scale winds were observed behind the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sensors recorded severe wind gusts as a thunderstorm moved through the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet, with the strongest gusts being 78 mph at Camel Back Mountain, 68 mph at English Village, and 62 mph at White Sage, Simpson Springs, and Fish Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More river flooding occurred across northern Utah in mid-May. The main rivers that were impacted were the Blacksmith Fork River and the South Fork of the Ogden River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blacksmith Fork River experienced flooding again in mid-May, peaking at 1434 cfs on May 16th, well above the flood flow of 934 cfs. Flooding was reported in Logan, Millville, and Hyrum. The bridge in Millville at 2900 South was washed out on both ends, and other low-lying bridges and roadways were impacted. The worst of the damage was at the Country Manor subdivision, where nine homes were flooded and evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More river flooding occurred across northern Utah in mid-May. The main rivers that were impacted were the Blacksmith Fork River and the South Fork of the Ogden River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the South Fork of the Ogden River from the Causey Reservoir to the Pineview Reservoir, with the gauge near Huntsville peaking at 1554 cfs on May 16th, well above the flood flow of 1019 cfs. This flooding impacted campgrounds and summer homes in and around Huntsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures and fast snowmelt caused widespread river flooding across Utah through the end of May. Major rivers impacted included the Sevier River, the South Fork of the Ogden River, and much of the Weber River system. Note that this episode continued into June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Weber River from the mouth of Weber Canyon downstream to Plain City. Only minor flooding occurred through the month of May, as the flood stage was mitigated, but this flooding began to damage the levee system at Plain City, which would eventually fail in June. Note that this event continued into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The windy April across Utah continued on the 13th, when another system moved through the area. The strongest winds were prefrontal winds across the western half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sensor in Centerfield, Utah recorded a maximum wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty southerly winds were observed across northern and western Utah ahead of an approaching storm system. The northern Wasatch Front also experienced gusty easterly canyon winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern Wasatch Front experienced both easterly canyon winds and southerly prefrontal winds. The maximum recorded wind gust was 52 mph at Hill Air Force Base, but damage due to winds was reported in multiple locations. In Brigham City, shingles were blown off a roof and several branches were broken off of trees. At the mouth of Weber Canyon, two separate trucks with camper trailers were knocked over due to strong winds coming out of the canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of experienced backcountry skiers was exploring a slope in Sanpete County when they triggered an avalanche, catching an carrying 3 people, and eventually killing one.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of very experienced backcountry skiers went to explore Big Horseshoe Bowl, an area near Ephraim, Utah, on the morning of March 26th. They had read the avalanche forecast, which had an overall hazard of Moderate with pockets of Considerable, so they were being careful, testing different slopes for avalanche activity before deciding if they should ski the slope. As they were testing one of these slopes, and about to decide that that particular slope was too dangerous, a large avalanche was triggered. ||The avalanche caught and carried 3 of the individuals. Two of these three were freed relatively quickly, but the third was completely buried. Using their beacons, they located him and eventually dug him out of the snow. They were able to revive him after extensive use of CPR, and the entire group was recovered by a SAR team later that day, but the individual died the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt from a record snowpack caused extended high flows and river flooding on the main stem of the Colorado and Green Rivers in Grand County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of June, high flows were already occurring and a flood advisories had already been issued for the Colorado River in Grand County. These advisories remained posted throughout the month due to near bankfull conditions, though a flood warning was briefly issued from June 7th to the 11th when the gauge near the Colorado state line went above the minor flood mark. Low land flooding was the main impact along the river from the Utah state line through Westwater Canyon and back into the Moab area. Several campgrounds and a bike path were closed along Highway 128 due to flood water inundation.  Minor flooding also occurred on private property along the Colorado River in Moab. The Green River rose to near bankfull conditions around June 6th but rose out of its banks to flood level on June 9th where it remained through the end of the month. The Green River crested around 16.5 feet between June 13th and 14th. Mitigation and reinforcement work was needed along levees in and near the Green River, including work to keep water from backing up into the downtown area of the town of Green River. Sand bagging and pumping of water along the golf course and a state park were necessary to keep flood waters at bay. The Green River eventually crossed an oxbow and into crop and ranch land, though mitigation work prevented many farms and ranches along the river from being flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah at the end of the year. This front produced strong winds in parts of northern Utah, beginning the evening of December 30th. Behind the cold front, conditions became favorable for a breaking wave event in eastern Utah, which produced strong winds into the afternoon of December 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred across the Great Salt Lake Desert and Mountains as the cold front moved through the area, then tapered off fairly quickly after the front exited the area. The strongest recorded wind gusts included 73 mph at Lakeside Mountain, 71 mph at Upper Cedar Mountain, 70 mph at Promontory Point, 61 mph at Wendover Peak, 60 mph at the SR-30 at Curlew and Hat Island sensors, and 58 mph at Gunnison Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly warming temperatures increased the runoff from a record snowpack over the Northeastern Utah mountains.  This resulted in an extended period of high flows along many area waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy runoff from record snowpack kept rivers and creeks across Unitah County running at elevated to over bankfull conditions through much of June. ||The Green River exceeded flood stage on June 1st at the Jensen river gage, briefly fell below this mark between the 2nd and the 3rd, and then returned to at least a minor flood stage through the end of the month. At it's peak, the Green River at Jensen rose to a moderate flood stage level from June 9th through June 20th. During these high flows, low land flooding was reported with some damage to irrigation equipment along the river banks. Oil and gas drilling had to be halted along the river due to the impact of the high water levels. Utah Highway 88 and Chipeta Grove Road was closed for over 2 weeks near Ouray. Up to a foot of water over Highway 88 was reported with 3 to 5 feet of water inundating Chipeta Creek Road. One residence near Ouray had to be evacuated due to encroaching flood water and another house had restricted access by the flood waters. One death was attributed to the increased flows when a kayak overturned and the kayaker could not be rescued.    ||As temperatures continued to climb so did area rivers draining from the Eastern Uinta Mountains. Areal advisories and warnings were issued for much of the month of June for the Duchesne, White, White Rocks and Uinta rivers. These statements also covered may local creeks and streams that were out of their banks. The largest impact was to agricultural interests near these waterways with lowland pasture, farmland and isolated structures becoming inundated with water. Livestock and equipment had to be moved away from the flooded areas. A drinking water line that supplies areas of Unitah County had to be repaired from high water damage along the Duchesne River. Several mitigation projects needed to be done along the Uinta and White Rocks rivers and water crossed many secondary roads along the Unita River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the storm system that brought heavy precipitation to Utah, a massive landslide occurred on the morning of Saturday, October 8th. This landslide caused significant damage along State Route 14 in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide occurred 8 miles east of Cedar City, along Utah State Route 14. The landslide occurred sometime before 5 a.m. on the morning of October 8th, when the slide was discovered and the road was closed by the Utah Department of Transportation. The landslide was 1,700 feet in length, and destroyed an 800 foot section of State Route 14. The dirt on the road was as much as 100 feet deep in some places, and it was estimated that the slide consisted of 1.5 million cubic yards of dirt, rock, and debris. This led to the closing of a 12-mile stretch of State Route 14, which is a widely used road in southern Utah, particularly for tourists trying to get to Cedar Breaks National Monument and Bryce Canyon National Park. Early estimates from the Utah Department of Transportation indicated that the cost to repair the damaged section of State Route 14 would be around $12 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific trough brushed northeast Utah where the mountains received significant snowfall and areas of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began on the last day of November and continued into the first day of December.  Snowfall amounts of 10 to 21 inches were measured above the 8000 foot level.  Wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph resulted in blowing and drifting snow at many locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Pacific trough brushed northeast Utah where the mountains received significant snowfall and areas of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began on the last day of November and continued into the first day of December. Snowfall amounts around 5 inches were measured. Wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph resulted in areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low dug south into southern Utah and Nevada on November 30th and December 1st. Combined with high pressure centered over Wyoming, a strong pressure gradient developed across much of Utah. This created strong northeasterly canyon and downslope winds across several parts of the state. These winds caused damage across the Wasatch Front, with most of the damage occurring along the northern Wasatch Front. In addition to the winds, heavy snow fell in the mountains of central and southern Utah as the low moved through the area. Note that this episode began in November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong and destructive downslope windstorm impacted the northern Wasatch Front, with maximum recorded wind gusts of 102 mph in Centerville, 92 mph in West Bountiful, 86 mph in Farmington, and numerous other reports in the 60 to 80 mph range.||Damage from this windstorm was substantial and widespread, with the most extensive damage occurring between North Salt Lake and South Ogden. UDOT reported that 11 semitrailers were knocked over along I-15, which eventually led them to close the road to semis for several hours. In Fruit Heights, a steeple was blown off the roof of a church. Hundreds of trees, power lines, and light poles were knocked over, and some of these caused additional damage when they landed on houses, cars, and other property. The winds were strong enough to shatter windows in some locations, as well as bend or uproot many metal road signs. In the area of the strongest winds, almost all homes experienced damage to roofs, fences, and siding. Flying debris caused a few injuries, but there were no fatalities associated with the wind. ||Monetary damage to infrastructure was calculated to be about $6.5 million, almost all of which occurred in Davis County. Private insured losses were a little over $75 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and associated cold front moved through the Great Basin on June 6th. Strong gusty winds were observed both ahead of and behind the cold front across much of western Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew across much of the West Desert through the late morning and afternoon. Maximum recorded wind gusts included 73 mph at Camel Back Mountain, 67 mph at Upper Cedar Mountain and V-Grid, 66 mph at Salt Flats and West Salt Flats, 65 mph at Causeway, 64 mph at Gunnison Island, 63 mph at Lakeside Mountain, and 62 mph at Playa Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable southerly flow led thunderstorm development with many areas receiving heavy rainfall and isolated severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds of 59 mph were measured and Canyonlands Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical moisture under a southwest flow aloft resulted in some thunderstorms with heavy rainfall in southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flood waters in Arches National Park were over a foot and a half deep across the Delicate Arch road which was closed for several hours until the water receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another warm-up across the state of Utah and another round of heavy rainfall led to increases in river flows during the middle of June. Impacts included continued flooding on the lower portions of the Weber River and new flooding along the Sevier and Logan Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Weber River continued through mid-June, then subsided once the levee near Plain City was fixed. Standing water continued to be a problem after the levee repairs, leaving residents with water-logged properties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another warm-up across the state of Utah and another round of heavy rainfall led to increases in river flows during the middle of June. Impacts included continued flooding on the lower portions of the Weber River and new flooding along the Sevier and Logan Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sevier River flooded again in mid-June, with the focus further south along the river this time. Low lying roads and bridges in the town of Hatch were impacted, including the Old Hatch Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another warm-up across the state of Utah and another round of heavy rainfall led to increases in river flows during the middle of June. Impacts included continued flooding on the lower portions of the Weber River and new flooding along the Sevier and Logan Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Logan River flooded again beginning in mid-June. The Logan River Golf Course and walking trail was partially closed when a breach in the river bank sent a large stream of water down the middle of the golf course. Damage occurred to a few low lying bridges, including the Millville Bridge and a railroad bridge in Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of thunderstorms formed across Utah on June 24th. The strongest of these storms moved through the Cache Valley, dropping large hail over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail with a diameter of one inch was reported in the Cache Valley near Trenton, Utah. The report was received via the NOAA Hazardous Weather Testbed - Experimental Warning Program Severe Hazards Analysis and Verification Experiment project.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty winds occurred across much of the state of Utah at the tail end of the month. A few isolated severe gusts were observed on June 28th, with stronger winds on June 29th. These winds were both synoptic-scale winds due to a storm system moving through the region, as well as due to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Logan ASOS recorded a maximum wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures were observed again at the end of June, leading to even more river flooding across Utah. Waterways that flooded significantly during this time period included Little Cottonwood Creek, the Duchesne River, and Spring Creek in Providence, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Cottonwood Creek reached flood stage for a couple days in late June, peaking at 916 cfs at Crestwood Park on the night of June 24th. Much of the flooding was mitigated, but some damage was observed along the creek. The culvert at 2000 East and 3500 South was severely damaged, resulting in an extended closure of the road. There was a road closure and some damage at 1200 West between 3300 South and 3400 South, after Little Cottonwood enters the Jordan River. Erosion and damage occurred to riverbanks and park areas along the creek, including Murray Park and Crestwood Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures were observed again at the end of June, leading to even more river flooding across Utah. Waterways that flooded significantly during this time period included Little Cottonwood Creek, the Duchesne River, and Spring Creek in Providence, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Logan River continued to flood through the end of June. This flooding damaged  summer homes in the Birch Glen area, and continued to significantly impact the Logan River Golf Course. Note that this event continued into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures were observed again at the end of June, leading to even more river flooding across Utah. Waterways that flooded significantly during this time period included Little Cottonwood Creek, the Duchesne River, and Spring Creek in Providence, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rivers in the Uinta Basin reached flood stage in late June, including Rock Creek, and the Duchesne River at a few different points. These floods were largely mitigated, but some minor damage was observed to low lying bridges and a few buildings near the banks of the rivers, including a few homes in Tabiona. Note that this event continued into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures were observed again at the end of June, leading to even more river flooding across Utah. Waterways that flooded significantly during this time period included Little Cottonwood Creek, the Duchesne River, and Spring Creek in Providence, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred on the Upper Weber River in late June due to the accelerated snowmelt, with the Weber at Oakley gauge peaking at 3133 cfs on June 30th. Much of this flooding was mitigated, but the high water did impact some buildings close to the river. Note that this event continued into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures were observed again at the end of June, leading to even more river flooding across Utah. Waterways that flooded significantly during this time period included Little Cottonwood Creek, the Duchesne River, and Spring Creek in Providence, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spring Creek near the town of Providence, Utah experienced flooding at the end of June. Most of the damage occurred when gravel clogged culverts along the creek, making the creek divert around the culverts, and causing damage to driveways and roads. At one point, flooding threatened houses, and a couple families were voluntarily evacuated. Throughout the event, a few houses along the creek experienced basement flooding. Note that this event continued into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded disturbance within a southerly flow of subtropical moisture across the region produced areas of heavy rainfall in portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across portions of Grand County in a short period of time, resulting in minor urban and small stream flooding.  Water up to 3 inches deep ran across Highway 191 about 15 miles northwest of Moab where Courthouse Wash runs under the road.  A rainfall total of 0.63 of an inch fell within 30 minutes in Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah on the 26th and 27th of July, producing several flash floods across the state. Strong winds were also reported with one of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy rainstorm produced flash flooding across parts of the Salt Lake Valley, and the hardest hit areas were Kearns, Taylorsville, and Murray. In Kearns, water was 9 to 12 inches deep across several roads, making them impassable. In Taylorsville, at least a half dozen homes had flooded basements near the intersection of 5400 South and 3200 West. Water caused a sinkhole to form in the roadway around 6200 South and 2700 West, buckling and collapsing the street. In Murray, approximately 25 homes were flooded, with some basements filling with 4 to 6 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first major monsoon surge of the summer moved into Utah on the second week of July. This moist airmass produced several flash floods, including multiple floods in Capitol Reef National Park. In northern Utah, where the lower levels of the atmosphere remained drier, some thunderstorms also produced severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding in Zion National Park stranded visitors along the North Fork of the Virgin River, specifically in the Orderville Canyon area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first major monsoon surge of the summer moved into Utah on the second week of July. This moist airmass produced several flash floods, including multiple floods in Capitol Reef National Park. In northern Utah, where the lower levels of the atmosphere remained drier, some thunderstorms also produced severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding occurred in Capitol Reef National Park. Water crossed the Scenic Drive in multiple locations from the visitors center to the Slickrock Divide, forcing rangers to close the road. In addition, flash flooding was reported in Sulphur Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah on the 26th and 27th of July, producing several flash floods across the state. Strong winds were also reported with one of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About an hour after a heavy rainstorm caused flash flooding in Salt Lake County, another storm moved through Utah County, causing flash flooding in Orem. Storm drains were overflowing and water covered the roads up to 3 inches deep. At least seven homes had flooding in their basements or garages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding threat began again in the middle of the month, with the largest flash floods in Capitol Reef National Park and along the Escalante River. Isolated large hail was reported with a couple of storms, and cloud-to-ground lightning struck a couple individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A construction worker, who was walking toward his truck at the Granite Construction Company supply site in West Haven, was thrown back from the truck when a bolt of lightning struck the vehicle. He was thrown several feet in the air and landed on his face, suffering facial injuries and hand abrasions. He would survive his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding threat began again in the middle of the month, with the largest flash floods in Capitol Reef National Park and along the Escalante River. Isolated large hail was reported with a couple of storms, and cloud-to-ground lightning struck a couple individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail with a diameter of approximately 0.75 inches was observed near Murray.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding threat began again in the middle of the month, with the largest flash floods in Capitol Reef National Park and along the Escalante River. Isolated large hail was reported with a couple of storms, and cloud-to-ground lightning struck a couple individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail with a diameter of 0.75 inches was reported in Escalante.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain led to several flash floods across southern Utah in the middle of September. A few of these floods were located in and around Zion National Park. One report of large hail was also received during this time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hikers who were caught in Crack Canyon during a thunderstorm reported hail that was approximately 1 inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding threat began again in the middle of the month, with the largest flash floods in Capitol Reef National Park and along the Escalante River. Isolated large hail was reported with a couple of storms, and cloud-to-ground lightning struck a couple individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding that began further north on the Escalante River eventually made its way downstream, with the strong flow of water finally dissipating when it reached Lake Powell. Note that this flooding began in Garfield County, with most of the rainfall also occurring in the basins further upstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding threat began again in the middle of the month, with the largest flash floods in Capitol Reef National Park and along the Escalante River. Isolated large hail was reported with a couple of storms, and cloud-to-ground lightning struck a couple individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light but steady rain over Capitol Reef National Park produced flash flooding in the Grand Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few rivers across Utah continued to flood into the month of July, before finally receding in mid-July. The major rivers that continued to flood included the Logan River and the Duchesne River. Note that this episode began in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Logan River continued to flood into the beginning of July. This flooding continued to damage and cause the partial closure of the Logan River Golf Course, but otherwise the impacts were largely mitigated, and the waters receded quickly as the snow began to run out. Note that this event began in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah on the 26th and 27th of July, producing several flash floods across the state. Strong winds were also reported with one of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported near State Route 95 in southern Emery County, with one of the roads near there being washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain led to several flash floods across southern Utah in the middle of September. A few of these floods were located in and around Zion National Park. One report of large hail was also received during this time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large flash floods were reported in Pine Creek and Clear Creek in Zion National Park, with flowing water as much as 7 to 8 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah on the 26th and 27th of July, producing several flash floods across the state. Strong winds were also reported with one of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along State Route 95, with large trees and rocks covering part of the road. In addition, damage was reported to State Route 276 going toward Bullfrog. Flash flooding also occurred along Poison Spring on the north aspect of the Henry Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park rangers in Capitol Reef National Park reported flash flooding along Sulphur Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding threat began again in the middle of the month, with the largest flash floods in Capitol Reef National Park and along the Escalante River. Isolated large hail was reported with a couple of storms, and cloud-to-ground lightning struck a couple individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Capitol Reef National Park, just south of the visitors center. Doppler radar indicated rainfall estimates of about an inch of water in 3 hours time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More thunderstorms formed across Utah on July 31st, leading to flash flooding and an isolated severe wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A visitor in Zion National Park reported a flash flood between Mountain of the Sun and the Twin Brothers. The flash flood was very short-lived.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SR-30 sensor recorded strong wind gusts for half an hour, with a maximum gust of 76 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast moving thunderstorm just north of Big Water created a large flash flood in the Wahweep drainage. This flood stranded two people for some time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few different sensors just east of Wendover recorded strong wind gusts, including 64 mph at the UDOT I-80 at Milepost 29 sensor, 60 mph at the West Salt Flats sensor, and 59 mph at the North Salt Flats sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding threat began again in the middle of the month, with the largest flash floods in Capitol Reef National Park and along the Escalante River. Isolated large hail was reported with a couple of storms, and cloud-to-ground lightning struck a couple individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personnel from the Grand Staircase Escalante-National Monument reported flash flooding along Alvey Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding threat began again in the middle of the month, with the largest flash floods in Capitol Reef National Park and along the Escalante River. Isolated large hail was reported with a couple of storms, and cloud-to-ground lightning struck a couple individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 51-year-old German tourist in Bryce Canyon National Park was hiking with his family along the Rim Trail near Sunset Point, when an isolated thunderstorm moved into the area. Most of the family ran toward their car, but the man sought refuge under a large Ponderosa Pine. A bolt of lightning stuck the tree, with some of the energy being transferred to the man, and he died at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah on the 26th and 27th of July, producing several flash floods across the state. Strong winds were also reported with one of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along River Road in Duchesne, and the Duchesne River quickly rose 800 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few rivers across Utah continued to flood into the month of July, before finally receding in mid-July. The major rivers that continued to flood included the Logan River and the Duchesne River. Note that this episode began in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rivers in the Uinta Basin remained at or near flood stage in early July, including Rock Creek, and the Duchesne River at a few different points. These floods were largely mitigated, but some minor damage was observed, including temporarily reducing access to potable water in Myton. A couple of vehicles were also severely damaged in the fast-flowing flood waters. Note that this event began in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flash flooding threat began again in the middle of the month, with the largest flash floods in Capitol Reef National Park and along the Escalante River. Isolated large hail was reported with a couple of storms, and cloud-to-ground lightning struck a couple individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred on the northern tributaries of the Escalante River, especially on North Creek and the North Fork of Birch Creek. This flooding eventually made its way down the Escalante, with a gauge along the Escalante quickly increasing to 1700 cfs. Note that flash flooding also occurred further downstream in Kane County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain led to several flash floods across southern Utah in the middle of September. A few of these floods were located in and around Zion National Park. One report of large hail was also received during this time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in Zion National Park along the East Fork of the Virgin River, with the gauge along this river rising to 1665 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain led to several flash floods across southern Utah in the middle of September. A few of these floods were located in and around Zion National Park. One report of large hail was also received during this time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Fort Pearce Wash caused flash flooding along that drainage, with the measured flow at the gauge going from 0 cfs to 763 cfs in a single hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed across northern Utah again on July 13th. One of these storms produced a major flash flood near Tabiona, which resulted in one fatality, while two other deaths occurred due to lightning strikes in the Scofield area and San Rafael Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Provo Municipal Airport reported a maximum wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms continued to form in a moist airmass across the state of Utah in the first few days of August. The strongest of these storms produced primarily severe wind gusts and hail in northern Utah, while producing heavy rain and flash flooding in southern and eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A participant of the Cooperative Observer Program, near Alpine, reported hail with a diameter of 0.75 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms formed in Utah in the middle of August, primarily across the northern half of the state. Most of these storms produced strong gusty winds, while a couple produced severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Interstate 80 sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a maximum wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed across northern Utah again on July 13th. One of these storms produced a major flash flood near Tabiona, which resulted in one fatality, while two other deaths occurred due to lightning strikes in the Scofield area and San Rafael Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving thunderstorm moved through the Scofield Scout Camp on the morning of July 13th. Lightning struck two of the scouts as they were trying to return to their campsite; one of them, a 12-year-old male, was killed. The other scout suffered burns, but survived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The return of monsoon moisture in the last week of August led to widespread showers and thunderstorms across the state of Utah, with some of these storms becoming severe or producing flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved over the Wendover area, with the Wendover Point of Entry sensor recording a maximum wind gust of 62 mph, and the Wendover Peak sensor recording a wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms continued to form in a moist airmass across the state of Utah in the first few days of August. The strongest of these storms produced primarily severe wind gusts and hail in northern Utah, while producing heavy rain and flash flooding in southern and eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Pleasant Creek, Sandy Creek, and Burro Wash in and near Capitol Reef National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed across northern Utah again on July 13th. One of these storms produced a major flash flood near Tabiona, which resulted in one fatality, while two other deaths occurred due to lightning strikes in the Scofield area and San Rafael Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In an area already experiencing river flooding, heavy rainfall caused flash flooding near Tabiona. The primary road damage was along State Route 35, where a gulch running under the road was heavily backed up for hundreds of yards, and the pressure from the backup eventually washed out the road. The resultant damage was an area 30 feet deep and 40 feet across where the road was entirely missing.||Around 11:45 pm MDT, two vehicles drove directly into the washed out area. The occupants of the first vehicle suffered one fatality and one injury.  The occupant of the other vehicle was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed across northern Utah again on July 13th. One of these storms produced a major flash flood near Tabiona, which resulted in one fatality, while two other deaths occurred due to lightning strikes in the Scofield area and San Rafael Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 56-year-old man was with his family near the Wedge Overlook in the San Rafael Desert when he was struck by lightning. CPR was performed by family members and emergency personnel who responded to the scene, but he was unable to be revived and died at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation across the region kept abnormally dry conditions in place across Southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions persisted across extreme southeast Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lack of widespread significant precipitation across the Four Corners region allowed abnormally dry conditions to return to Southeast Utah in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions moved back into extreme Southeast Utah with below normal precipitation reported across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The return of monsoon moisture in the last week of August led to widespread showers and thunderstorms across the state of Utah, with some of these storms becoming severe or producing flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over Pleasant Creek in Capitol Reef National Park caused flash flooding. This flooding ran downstream to the Fremont River, which was measured at an increased flow of 1296 cfs near Caineville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions in extreme southeast Utah were alleviated during January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation in January was limited over southeast Utah, although it was sufficient to ease the abnormally dry conditions in that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain led to several flash floods across southern Utah in the middle of September. A few of these floods were located in and around Zion National Park. One report of large hail was also received during this time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Fortymile Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain led to several flash floods across southern Utah in the middle of September. A few of these floods were located in and around Zion National Park. One report of large hail was also received during this time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along the Paria River drainage. The gauge on the Paria near Kanab went from 185 cfs to 1212 cfs in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain led to several flash floods across southern Utah in the middle of September. A few of these floods were located in and around Zion National Park. One report of large hail was also received during this time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in many locations of Zion National Park, with floods reported over a large portion of the southern half of the park. Pine Creek and Spry Canyon both experienced large floods, with water in Pine Creek reportedly 6 to 7 feet high. Large rocks and other debris impacted some of the roads around the park. In addition, many of the smaller drainages that flow south to Rockville experienced flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain led to several flash floods across southern Utah in the middle of September. A few of these floods were located in and around Zion National Park. One report of large hail was also received during this time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Crack Canyon, Little Wildhorse Canyon, and other washes near the Little Wildhorse Canyon trailhead. The floods were generally large and hikers caught in Crack Canyon had to run from the water, eventually making it back to the road before the water caught up with them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The return of monsoon moisture in the last week of August led to widespread showers and thunderstorms across the state of Utah, with some of these storms becoming severe or producing flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three separate sensors in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded strong wind gusts with a passing thunderstorm, with a 66 mph gust observed at both the Camel Back Mountain site and Tower Grid site, as well as a 59 mph gust reported at the Ditto sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The return of monsoon moisture in the last week of August led to widespread showers and thunderstorms across the state of Utah, with some of these storms becoming severe or producing flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Causeway sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a maximum wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado was spotted in far southern Utah on August 24th, and flash flooding occurred once again in Capitol Reef National Park on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along Sulpher Creek in Capitol Reef National Park; the flood was categorized as impressive by one of the park rangers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado was spotted in far southern Utah on August 24th, and flash flooding occurred once again in Capitol Reef National Park on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter observed a tornado west of Apple Valley, Utah. The weak tornado was short-lived and caused no damage; tall grasses in the area were disturbed, but nearby bushes and trees remained undamaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing heavy rain led to several flash floods across southern Utah in the middle of September. A few of these floods were located in and around Zion National Park. One report of large hail was also received during this time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rainfall fell over a broad area near Capitol Reef National Park. This initially caused flash flooding along Sulphur Creek and other parts of Capitol Reef. Eventually this water made its way downstream, causing flash flooding along the Dirty Devil River, which went from 538 to 1419 cfs in a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific trough produced significant snowfall in the mountains of northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 7 inches was measured across the area.  Winds gusted 25 to 35 mph and produced local blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific trough produced significant snowfall in the mountains of northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall around 5 inches was measured across the area. Winds gusted 20 to 30 mph and produced local blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southwest flow aloft with embedded disturbances resulted in heavy snow for most mountain areas and significant snowfall in the Uintah Basin of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 12 to 18 inches were measured near and above the 8000 foot level.  Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southwest flow aloft with embedded disturbances resulted in heavy snow for most mountain areas and significant snowfall in the Uintah Basin of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 12 to 14 inches were measured near and above the 8000 foot level.  Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southwest flow aloft with embedded disturbances resulted in heavy snow for most mountain areas and significant snowfall in the Uintah Basin of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 5 to 12 inches were measured near and above the 8000 foot level.  Wind gusts of 40 to 80 mph resulted in localized blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southwest flow aloft with embedded disturbances resulted in heavy snow for most mountain areas and significant snowfall in the Uintah Basin of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were measured across the area.  Wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph produced localized blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved eastward across the area and produced significant snowfall in some mountain areas of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations around 5 inches were measured across the area for the duration of this event which continued into February.  Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph produced localized blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved eastward across the area and produced significant snowfall in some mountain areas of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were measured across the area for the duration of this event which continued into February.  Wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved eastward across the area and produced significant snowfall in some mountain areas of eastern Utah. The Pacific low was quickly followed by Arctic air and gusty winds that spread southward across northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were measured across the area for the duration of this event which began in January. Wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved eastward across the area and produced significant snowfall in some mountain areas of eastern Utah. The Pacific low was quickly followed by Arctic air and gusty winds that spread southward across northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations around 5 inches were measured across the area for the duration of this event which began in January.  Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph produced localized blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved eastward across the area and produced significant snowfall in some mountain areas of eastern Utah. The Pacific low was quickly followed by Arctic air and gusty winds that spread southward across northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranged from 25 degrees below zero to 40 degrees below zero Fahrenheit, as winds gusted 25 to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved eastward across the area and produced significant snowfall in some mountain areas of eastern Utah. The Pacific low was quickly followed by Arctic air and gusty winds that spread southward across northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranged from 30 degrees below zero to 50 degrees below zero Fahrenheit, as winds gusted 25 to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved eastward across the area and produced significant snowfall in some mountain areas of eastern Utah. The Pacific low was quickly followed by Arctic air and gusty winds that spread southward across northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranged from 30 degrees below zero to 45 degrees below zero Fahrenheit, as winds gusted 20 to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough moved across the Northern Rockies and resulted in significant snowfall in the mountains of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 9 inches was measured at elevations above the 8000 foot level.  Winds gusted 30 to 55 mph and created areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty southerly winds were observed across northern and western Utah ahead of an approaching storm system. The northern Wasatch Front also experienced gusty easterly canyon winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum wind gusts across the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys included 66 mph at South Mountain, 65 mph at Vernon Hill, 63 mph at Stockton Bar, and 59 mph at Baccus Highway. With these strong southerly winds, a truck with a camper trailer was blown over on Interstate 80 at mile marker 107.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front initiated widespread thunderstorms across northern Utah, with many of them producing severe wind gusts and causing property damage. In addition, some strong synoptic-scale winds were observed behind the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ditto sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a maximum wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front initiated widespread thunderstorms across northern Utah, with many of them producing severe wind gusts and causing property damage. In addition, some strong synoptic-scale winds were observed behind the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lake Point sensor recorded a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures along with occasional heavy rain led to continued flooding across the state of Utah. Major rivers that experienced flooding included the Weber River, the Sevier River, and the South Fork of the Ogden River. Note that this episode began in May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sevier River at Sigurd peaked at 1728 cfs on June 1st, then remained above flood stage for more than a week after that as it slowly receded. Many roads across Sevier county continued to be impacted and damaged, including State Road 118 between Joseph and Monroe, the Sevier River Road between Central Valley and Annabella, and Seegmiller Lane near Richfield. In addition, flood waters seeped into about a dozen homes, while threatening more than a dozen others.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High flows along area rivers and streams continued through the first part of July as the record snow pack continued to melt across the higher terrain of Northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The main stem of the Green River remained under flood conditions through July 4th, followed by lowering water levels through July 13th and included the stretch of the river from the confluence with the Yampa to near Ouray, Utah. The main impacts to the high flow was to agricultural interests due to damage of irrigation equipment and the inundation of ranch and pasture land. Oil and gas drilling operations were also negatively impacted by the high flows through early July. Smaller rivers and creeks continued to run above bankfull conditions through around July 9th, though the addition of rainfall in the middle of the month kept these waterways running high through late July. Again, the main impact was to pasture and ranch land near these rivers.
